cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to work through this problem step by step. So, we have a fruit stand that sells five fruits: apples, oranges, watermelons, pears, and mangoes. The problem gives us some information about the prices, and we need to determine which of the options is correct. First, let me list out all the given information: 1. Apples are more expensive than oranges. (A > O) 2. Oranges are more expensive than mangoes. (O > M) 3. Apples are the third-most expensive. So, there are two fruits more expensive than apples and two less expensive than apples. 4. Pears are more expensive than watermelons. (P > W) Our goal is to find out which of the options (A-E) is correct based on these constraints. Let me try to create an ordering of the fruits from most expensive to least expensive. Let me denote the fruits as A (apples), O (oranges), W (watermelons), P (pears), and M (mangoes). From point 3, apples are the third-most expensive. That means there are two fruits more expensive than apples and two fruits less expensive than apples. From point 1, apples are more expensive than oranges (A > O). And from point 2, oranges are more expensive than mangoes (O > M). So we have A > O > M. So apples are more expensive than oranges, which are more expensive than mangoes.
